---
title: "Headache in Pregnancy"
category: "Headache"
section_id: "headache-pregnancy"
last_updated: "2026-07-01"
source: "UF Neurology Resident Handbook 2026-2027"
---

# Headache in Pregnancy

HEADACHE IN PREGNANCY

**Primary Headaches**

1)  Migraine headache:  in general, retrospective studies have suggested that migraine frequency and severity **decrease** during pregnancy, particularly during the 2nd and 3rd trimesters.  A prospective study of 49 patients showed 79% had complete remission of headaches by the third trimester. By one month postpartum, 50% had recurrence of migraine.

2)  Tension Headaches:  no change in frequency during pregnancy, but few data exist

**Secondary Headaches**

- Pseudotumor cerebri (IIH):  see other section

- Pre-eclampsia: new hypertension >140/90,  proteinuria>300mg/dL; headache may precede development of serious neurologic sequelae (i.e. eclampsia with seizures)

- Subarachnoid hemorrhage: incidence may be higher in pregnancy (between 0.9-5/10,000) and during the six weeks postpartum, risk of SAH increases 28-fold

- Cerebral Venous Sinus Thrombosis: occurs in 1/2500-10,000 deliveries; especially likely if associated hypercoagulable states (protein C/S or antithrombin III deficiency, antiphospholipid antibody syndrome). Can occur up to 2-8 weeks postpartum, too!

- Postdural Puncture/Nonpostdural puncture: can occur with epidural & spinal anesthesia (CSF leak, pneumocephalus; other complications-subdural hematoma, aseptic/septic meningitis)

Workup:

1)  Worrisome features: new-onset headache, change in pattern, visual changes, speech abnormalities, focal neuro signs/sxs, meningeal signs, trauma, seizure, history of malignancy

2)  Detailed headache history & neurologic ROS; medications, diet

3)  Vitals, neuro exam (including funduscopic exam)

4)  Labs (urinalysis, pre-eclampsia labs)

5)  Imaging: if you suspect SAH → non-contrast head CT (sensitivity ~90% in first 24 h of onset);

it takes 50-100 head CTs to reach a concerning level of radiation exposure (5 rads!)

          	 if you suspect venous sinus thrombosis →  MRI/MRV (although, if necessary, a CT

venogram can be done).  The National Radiological Protection Board advises against

MRI in the first trimester, but there is no evidence to support the advisory.

6)  LP: if imaging reveals no hemorrhage and the suspicion remains high, lumbar puncture is the only way to exclude SAH.  Also indicated if suspicious of meningitis.

Treatment  in Pregnant Patients: What's Safe?  Usually decided by our OB/GYN colleagues…

| Category B – no human studies, but animal studies show no risk | Category C - no human/ animal studies or animal studies show adverse effect | Category D - evidence of risk to humans based on animal or human studies | Category X  - evidence of risk to fetuses outweighs any benefit, contraindicated |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| acetaminophen, caffeine, ibuprofen*, naproxen*, indomethacin, butorphanol, meperidine, oxycodone, hydromorphone, morphine,          dichloralphenazone, Reglan (metoclopramide), fluoxetine | aspirin*, codeine, hydrocodone, propoxyphene, butalbital, sumitriptan,   prochlorperazine promethazine, clonazepam | lorazepam, diazepam,  amitriptyline, imipramine, nortriptyline * avoided in third trimester | ergotamine, dihydroergotamine |
